nativeRange.habitat: Shallow riverbanks and marshy margins where it grows both emersed and submersed

decorPreferences: midground placement in dense groupings of multiple stems
nutrient-rich aquasoil substrate preferred for fuller growth and stronger color
open sightlines and stable lighting help maintain compact, well-colored form

avoidWith: plant-eating fish such as goldfish, silver dollars, or large herbivorous cichlids

summary: Hygrophila sp. 'Araguaia' is a smaller, narrow-leaved Hygrophila grown for its distinctive elongated lance-shaped leaves that develop bronze, red, and purple tones under strong light, CO2, and iron-rich fertilization. Best used in dense midground clusters; rated intermediate care.
careNotes: Hygrophila sp. 'Araguaia' is a compact, narrow-leaved stem plant valued for its strap-like leaves and the bronze-to-reddish coloration that develops under good conditions. Despite the trade name's reference to Brazil's Rio Araguaia, taxonomic work (cited by Flowgrow) indicates the plant is almost certainly an Asian species — likely an undescribed Hygrophila or a form of Hygrophila lancea — and the South American origin should be treated as marketing rather than fact. It is generally placed in the midground and works well in tight groupings of several stems; in smaller tanks it can also serve as a low background. Light demand is on the high side: Flowgrow rates it 'high' demand, and Modern Aquarium notes that stronger light improves both compactness and color. CO2 is not strictly required but is strongly recommended (Flowgrow reports 25-40 mg/l) for the dense, well-colored growth this plant is sold for. A nutrient-rich substrate (aquasoil) plus regular liquid fertilization gives the best results, and iron supplementation in particular brings out red/bronze tones. Tolerates a broad temperature window (~18-30°C per Flowgrow); the 22-28°C range above reflects the recommended sweet spot. Growth rate is moderate — faster under high light and CO2, slower in low-tech setups, where leaves also tend to stay greener and more elongated. The plant grows readily emersed and is often shipped that way; submerged-grown shoots are typically more compact. Minimum tank volume of ~40 L is a reasonable guideline for displaying a small midground stand; no source specified an exact figure.
breedingNotes: Propagation is by stem cuttings: trim a healthy top 5-10 cm long, strip the lowest leaves, and replant directly into the substrate. New roots and lateral shoots form readily. Regular topping and replanting of cuttings encourages a denser, bushier stand and is the standard way to thicken a group.

primary.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided taxonomy (Acanthaceae, Hygrophila lancea Miq.), origin caveat ('allegedly Brazil, Rio Araguaia; likely Asia' per Smithsonian botanist), temperature 18-30°C (optimum 24-28°C), pH 4.5-7, kH 0-7 dKH, CO2 25-40 mg/l, high light demand, medium growth, propagation by cuttings, emersed-growth capability, and medium difficulty.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked husbandry: temperature 72-82°F (~22-28°C), moderate-to-strong light improves compactness and color, CO2 recommended but not required, moderate-to-fast growth, midground placement (low background in small tanks), propagation by trimming and replanting tops, nutrient-rich substrate, iron supplementation for color, moderate difficulty, bronze-purple tones under optimal conditions.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Hygrophila genus)
additional.notes: Used for genus-level taxonomy (family Acanthaceae, order Lamiales) and confirmation that 'Araguaia' is treated as a cultivar of Hygrophila lancea in aquarium use, supporting the Asian-origin reading over the Brazilian trade story.
